rows.answer:
**My best single-number estimate: 45%.**

That is: I’d put about a **45% probability** on AGI being achieved by 2035 by a system whose core lineage is recognizably **LLM / large foundation-model-based** rather than something architecturally discontinuous.

A rough decomposition:

- **Probability of AGI by 2035 at all:** ~55–60%  
- **Conditional probability that, if AGI happens by then, it comes from LLM-like/foundation-model systems:** ~70–80%  
- Multiplying those gives roughly **40–50%**, so my point estimate is **45%**.

Key reasons I don’t put it much lower:

- LLMs have shown surprisingly broad generalization from scale, data, RL/post-training, tool use, and multimodality.
- The main industrial effort, talent, compute, and capital are currently concentrated on scaling and extending LLM-like models.
- “LLM” systems by 2035 will likely include memory, agents, search, tool use, synthetic data, RL, multimodal world modeling, and possibly robotics interfaces. That may be enough without a clean architectural break.
- Many alleged LLM weaknesses — math, coding, planning, factuality, long-context use — have been improving via engineering and training, not requiring an entirely new paradigm so far.

Reasons I don’t put it much higher:

- Current LLMs still have major weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon autonomy, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and robust reasoning.
- Scaling may hit harder limits from data, compute, energy, economics, or diminishing returns.
- A future AGI system may be hybrid enough that calling it “an LLM” becomes misleading.
- A genuinely different architecture — e.g. more explicitly agentic world models, neurosymbolic systems, embodied-learning systems, or new memory/planning architectures — could overtake LLMs.

So my view is: **Gary Marcus-style “game over” confidence seems too strong**, but so does the opposite view that LLMs are almost guaranteed to get us there. If “LLM” means *vanilla text-only next-token prediction*, my estimate would be far lower, maybe **10–15%**. But if it means *LLM-descended multimodal foundation-model systems with tools, memory, RL, and agent scaffolding*, then my estimate is **45% by 2035**.

rows.answer:
**My best single-number estimate: 45%.**

By that I mean: roughly a **45% chance that by 2035 some lab builds a system reasonably describable as AGI, and that the decisive path is recognizably LLM/foundation-model-descended** — e.g. multimodal transformer-like models plus post-training, tool use, memory, planning/search, RL, synthetic data, and agent scaffolding — rather than a truly separate paradigm such as a mainly symbolic, brain-inspired, or non-language-centric RL/world-model architecture.

Why that high:

- **LLMs/foundation models are currently the only demonstrated general-purpose AI substrate** with broad competence across language, code, reasoning, knowledge work, vision-language tasks, and tool use.
- **The economic and engineering momentum is enormous.** The biggest labs, chips, data pipelines, benchmarks, and deployment loops are all centered on this paradigm.
- **Many apparent LLM weaknesses may be patchable without a paradigm break:** retrieval for memory, tools for calculation, verifiers for reasoning, agents for task execution, multimodal grounding, RL/post-training for behavior, and inference-time search for harder problems.
- If AGI arrives relatively soon, it is more likely to come from the dominant current paradigm than from a still-unproven replacement.

Why not higher:

- **Current LLMs remain brittle** on long-horizon planning, causal reasoning, autonomous learning, robust truthfulness, and real-world agency.
- Scaling may face **data, compute, cost, energy, and reliability bottlenecks**.
- “LLM” may stop being the right description if the winning system becomes a persistent world-model agent trained mostly through interaction, simulation, RL, or self-play, with language only as one interface.
- There is also a substantial chance that **no system meeting a strong AGI bar exists by 2035**.

So my view is: **Gary Marcus is too confident if he means “LLMs definitely cannot get us there.”** But LLM-based AGI by 2035 is also far from guaranteed. The most likely successful near-term route, if there is one, is not “just scale a chatbot,” but an **LLM-descended hybrid cognitive architecture**.

My best single-number estimate: **40%**.

By that I mean: **a roughly 40% chance that by the end of 2035 some lab achieves AGI and that the decisive system is LLM/foundation-model–descended**, not a wholly different paradigm.

Key assumptions:

- **“Via LLMs”** includes transformer-like or LLM-derived multimodal foundation models with tool use, memory, retrieval, RL/post-training, planning/search, synthetic data, and agent scaffolding.
- It does **not** mean “just scale next-token prediction with no architectural or training additions.”
- **“AGI”** means broadly human-level cognitive capability across most economically relevant intellectual tasks, with robust autonomy and learning, not necessarily superintelligence or fully human-equivalent robotics.

Why not lower:

- LLMs are currently the most general AI systems we have.
- Scaling, post-training, tool use, code execution, long context, multimodality, and test-time compute all seem capable of addressing some current weaknesses.
- The money, talent, infrastructure, and institutional momentum are overwhelmingly concentrated on LLM/foundation-model descendants.
- If AGI happens relatively soon, path dependence strongly favors the current paradigm.

Why not higher:

- Current LLMs still have serious issues with reliability, long-horizon agency, grounding, continual learning, causal/world modeling, and out-of-distribution robustness.
- It is plausible that these are not just engineering gaps but signs that LLMs lack some key ingredients.
- Compute, data, power, regulation, or economics may slow progress.

rows.answer:
**My best single-number estimate: 45%.**

I interpret the question as: *By the end of 2035, what is the probability that a generally recognized AGI is achieved and that its core is in the LLM/foundation-model lineage, rather than a fundamentally different paradigm?* On that interpretation, I’d put it at **~45%**.

A rough decomposition behind that number:

- **~55%** chance that something reasonably called AGI is achieved by 2035.
- Conditional on that happening, **~80%** chance the winning system is LLM/foundation-model-descended rather than a clean break.
- That gives **~45%** unconditional probability for “AGI via LLMs by 2035.”

I would break the remaining probability roughly as:

- **45%**: AGI via LLM/foundation-model lineage by 2035  
- **10%**: AGI via fundamentally different architecture by 2035  
- **45%**: no widely recognized AGI by 2035  

By “via LLMs,” I do **not** mean a plain 2023-style chatbot scaled up. I mean a system whose central learned cognitive engine is recognizably descended from large pretrained language/multimodal models: transformer-like or successor sequence models, trained on text/code/multimodal data, then augmented with tools, memory, search, RL, synthetic data, verifiers, long-horizon agents, etc.

Why I put it this high:

- LLMs have already shown unusually broad transfer across language, code, reasoning, tutoring, planning, and tool use.
- The largest labs, talent pools, data pipelines, hardware stacks, and product ecosystems are heavily optimized around this paradigm.
- Many apparent LLM weaknesses may be system-level rather than paradigm-level: retrieval helps memory; tools help calculation; verifiers help reliability; search/test-time compute helps reasoning; agents help long-horizon tasks.
- If AGI arrives by 2035, path dependence strongly favors the currently dominant scalable paradigm.

Why I do **not** put it at 70–90%:

- Current LLMs still struggle with robust long-horizon autonomy, causal understanding, continual learning, grounding, and reliability under distribution shift.
- It is unclear whether next-token/masked-token style training plus RL and scaffolding can produce stable agency rather than impressive but brittle imitation.
- Data and compute scaling may hit economic, physical, regulatory, or diminishing-return limits.
- A future “AGI” may require more explicit world models, active learning, memory architectures, planning modules, or embodied interaction than today’s LLM paradigm naturally supplies.
- The boundary is fuzzy: a future system may include an LLM as interface but rely on a different cognitive core.

So my view is: **Gary Marcus may be right that today’s LLMs are not, by themselves, the royal road to AGI. But “LLM-descended systems plus major architectural/system-level additions” still look like the leading candidate for AGI by 2035.**

Final answer: **45%.**

rows.answer:
**Best single-number estimate: ~40%.**

By that I mean: **about a 40% chance that by the end of 2035 some lab achieves broadly human-level, general-purpose AI and that the central route is LLM/foundation-model–based**, not a wholly different paradigm. If you instead mean *conditional on AGI being achieved by 2035*, I’d put the LLM-centered route higher, roughly in the **60–70%** range.

Why not higher?

- **LLMs have real generality**: language/multimodal foundation models already show broad transfer, coding ability, tool use, reasoning-like behavior, and can be embedded in agentic systems with memory, search, verifiers, robotics interfaces, etc.
- **The ecosystem is massively committed**: compute infrastructure, talent, capital, data pipelines, and product feedback are all concentrated around scaling and augmenting LLM-like systems.
- **“LLM” will likely blur**: future systems may not be pure next-token predictors. They may include planning, retrieval, RL, world models, memory, simulation, and tool use. But if a transformer/foundation-model core remains the main cognitive substrate, I’d still count that as “via LLMs.”
- **Near-term AGI, if it happens, is likelier to build on the current winning paradigm** than to come from a clean-sheet cognitive architecture.

Why not much higher?

- **Reliability and agency remain hard**: hallucination, brittle reasoning, long-horizon planning, autonomous learning, causal modeling, and robust self-correction are not solved just by current LLM scaling.
- **Data/compute scaling may hit limits**: high-quality data, inference cost, energy, and diminishing returns could slow progress.
- **AGI may require deeper architectural changes**: persistent memory, grounded world models, active learning, model-based RL, symbolic abstraction, or embodied interaction may be essential enough that the resulting system is no longer meaningfully “an LLM.”
- **2035 is close**: a decade is long in AI, but short for solving all the pieces needed for robust general intelligence.

So my rough decomposition would be: AGI by 2035 is plausible but far from guaranteed; if it does arrive that soon, it is more likely than not to be through **LLM-descended foundation models plus scaffolding**, not through an unrelated architecture. rows.answer:
**Best single-number estimate: 35%.**

That is my estimate for the **unconditional probability** that by **2035**, some lab/company achieves something reasonably describable as AGI and that the central route is **LLM-centered systems** rather than a fundamentally different paradigm.

My rough decomposition:

- **P(AGI by 2035 at all): ~45–50%**
- **P(LLM-centered route | AGI by 2035): ~65–75%**
- Combined: roughly **0.47 × 0.70 ≈ 33%**, rounded to **35%**

By “LLM-centered,” I would include systems that are not “just chatbots,” but are built around scaled language/multimodal foundation models with tool use, memory, planning modules, RL/post-training, synthetic data, long-context reasoning, agent scaffolding, etc. I would not count a mostly non-language-native architecture — e.g. a new model-based RL/cognitive architecture/world-model system where language is only an interface.

Why not higher?

- Current LLMs still struggle with reliability, grounded world modeling, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and robust causal reasoning.
- Scaling may face compute, data, energy, economic, and regulatory limits.
- Some missing pieces may require more than “bigger transformer + tools.”
- “AGI” itself may require embodiment, persistent agency, or learning dynamics that current LLM training does not naturally provide.

Why not lower?

- LLMs have already demonstrated surprising generality and are becoming multimodal, agentic, tool-using, and increasingly capable at coding/reasoning.
- The economic incentive is enormous, and many labs are pushing along this trajectory.
- Future “LLMs” may absorb ideas from RL, world modeling, search, memory, and symbolic reasoning while still being recognizably foundation-model-centered.
- If AGI arrives by 2035, it seems more likely to evolve from the dominant, heavily funded paradigm than from a clean-sheet alternative.

So my view is: **Gary Marcus may be right that plain LLM scaling is not enough**, but I would not conclude “game over.” The more plausible AGI path, if it happens by 2035, is a **hybrid LLM-centered architecture**. My best estimate for that full event is **35%**.

rows.answer:
**My best single-number estimate: 40%.**

That is, I’d put about a **40% probability** on AGI being achieved by end-2035 via an **LLM-descended/foundation-model path** — including multimodal models, tool use, agent scaffolding, memory, RL fine-tuning, synthetic data, verifiers, code execution, etc. — rather than via a fundamentally different paradigm.

A useful decomposition of my view:

| Outcome by 2035 | Probability |
|---|---:|
| No AGI by 2035 | ~45% |
| AGI via LLM/foundation-model lineage | ~40% |
| AGI via fundamentally different architecture | ~15% |

So **conditional on AGI happening by 2035**, I’d say there’s roughly a **70–75% chance** it comes from the LLM/foundation-model lineage.

Why I’m not as dismissive as Gary Marcus:

- **LLMs already capture a lot of generality**: language, coding, reasoning, tool use, multimodal grounding, and instruction following are all central to human cognitive work.
- **The research frontier is not “just next-token prediction” anymore**: frontier systems increasingly combine LLMs with RL, search, memory, retrieval, planning loops, agents, verifiers, multimodal perception, and external tools.
- **Incumbent advantage matters**: the money, talent, chips, infrastructure, datasets, and deployment pipelines are overwhelmingly concentrated around foundation-model labs.
- **Architectural evolution may look incremental but be sufficient**: if transformers/LLMs acquire better memory, planning, self-correction, and world modeling, people may still reasonably call the result “LLM-based.”

Why I’m not much higher than 40%:

- **Reliability and long-horizon agency remain weak**: current models still struggle with robust planning, self-monitoring, causal understanding, and low-error execution over long tasks.
- **Scaling may hit bottlenecks**: data quality, compute cost, energy, inference expense, and diminishing returns could slow progress.
- **AGI may require stronger forms of continual learning, embodiment, or world modeling** than today’s LLM-centered systems naturally provide.
- **A successor paradigm could absorb LLMs but no longer be meaningfully “an LLM”** — e.g. a new kind of learned world-model agent, neurosymbolic system, or RL/self-play architecture.

If “LLM” means **strictly a larger text-only chatbot trained mostly by next-token prediction**, my estimate would be much lower. But if it means **the current foundation-model lineage evolving into multimodal, tool-using, agentic systems**, then my answer is: **40% by 2035**.

rows.answer:
**Best single-number estimate: 40%.**

I’m interpreting “via LLMs” broadly: not a bare text-only next-token chatbot, but an LLM/foundation-model-centered system—likely multimodal, tool-using, agentic, with memory, retrieval, verifiers, RL, planning/search, and maybe robotics interfaces—where the core general intelligence comes from the LLM lineage rather than a clean architectural break.

Why I’d put it around **40%**:

- **Strong evidence for the LLM path:** Scaling has produced surprisingly general capabilities; language/code are rich training media; tool use, multimodality, long context, synthetic data, verifiers, and test-time compute can plausibly patch many current weaknesses. The empirical lead and investment behind LLM-style systems are enormous.
- **But not “game over”:** Current LLMs still struggle with reliability, grounded causal models, long-horizon autonomy, continual learning, robust planning, and out-of-distribution reasoning. It is not obvious that more scale plus scaffolding fully solves those.
- **2035 is soon but not extremely soon:** There is enough time for several generations of models, compute growth, better data pipelines, agent training, and architecture refinements—but also enough possibility of hitting bottlenecks in data, energy, economics, regulation, or capability plateaus.
- **Alternative architectures remain plausible:** A future AGI might be more model-based RL, world-model-centric, neuro-symbolic, brain-inspired, or built around active embodied learning. My guess is these are less likely to beat the LLM/foundation-model paradigm by 2035, but they are not negligible.
- **Definitions matter a lot:** If “LLM” means “pure autoregressive text model with no tools, memory, RL, search, or multimodal grounding,” my estimate would be much lower. If “LLM” means “the current transformer/foundation-model paradigm, heavily augmented,” then **40%** is my best unconditional estimate.

So my bottom line: **Gary Marcus may be right that today’s LLMs are not sufficient as-is, but I would not call the LLM/foundation-model route dead. I’d give it roughly a 40% chance of producing AGI by 2035.**

rows.answer:
**Best single-number estimate: 40%.**

That is my estimate for the event: **by the end of 2035, some lab achieves AGI using an LLM-centered or LLM-descended architecture**, rather than a fundamentally different paradigm.

If you meant **conditional on AGI being achieved by 2035 at all**, then my estimate would be higher: roughly **70%** that the winning system is LLM/foundation-model-descended.

My rough decomposition:

- **~55%** chance some form of AGI is achieved by 2035.
- **~70%** chance, conditional on that, that it comes from LLM/foundation-model lineage.
- Product: **~0.55 × 0.70 ≈ 0.40**.

Why not higher?

- Current LLMs still have serious weaknesses: hallucination, long-horizon planning, causal reasoning, robustness, continual learning, and autonomous error correction.
- “Scaling plus RL plus tools” may not be enough; future systems may need stronger world models, memory, active learning, formal reasoning, or embodiment.
- The label “LLM” may become blurry: a 2035 AGI might include language models, vision models, planners, verifiers, tool-use loops, retrieval, simulation, and RL. At some point it may be more accurate to call it a general foundation-model agent than a mere LLM.

Why not lower?

- The frontier AI ecosystem is heavily path-dependent: the most capital, talent, infrastructure, and empirical progress are currently concentrated around transformer/foundation-model systems.
- LLMs already provide a surprisingly general interface for reasoning, coding, tool use, instruction following, and multimodal integration.
- Many proposed “fixes” to LLM limitations—retrieval, agents, self-play, verifiers, synthetic data, test-time search, memory, multimodality—can be added around an LLM core without abandoning the paradigm.
- If AGI arrives relatively soon, by 2035, it is more likely to be an extension of the current dominant approach than a totally new architecture discovered, scaled, and industrialized from scratch.

So my view is: **Gary Marcus may be right that today’s LLMs alone are not sufficient, but I would not conclude that LLM-descended systems are a dead end.** My median expectation, conditional on AGI arriving by 2035, is that it looks like a heavily augmented foundation-model agent, not a cleanly separate non-LLM architecture.

**Best single-number estimate: ~35%** that **AGI is achieved by 2035 via LLM-descended systems** by some lab/company.

- **P(LLM/foundation-model-centered route | AGI by 2035): ~65–75%**

- Current LLMs still struggle with reliability, long-horizon agency, causal/world modeling, robust planning, self-correction, and hallucination.

- “AGI” may require architectural ingredients beyond today’s LLM paradigm: persistent memory, active learning, explicit planning/search, grounded world models, neurosymbolic systems, or embodied interaction.

- The strongest commercial and research momentum is currently around foundation-model systems, so if AGI arrives soon, it is likely to come from that lineage.
- Many “non-LLM” improvements may end up being incorporated into LLM-based agents rather than replacing them.
- Language/code-trained models provide a powerful interface to human knowledge, software tools, and self-improvement loops.

So my bottom line is:

**35% unconditional probability that AGI is achieved by 2035 through an LLM/foundation-model-centered path.**

If you instead mean: “conditional on AGI being achieved by 2035, will the winning path be LLM-based rather than fundamentally different?” then my estimate is about **70%**.

rows.answer:
My best single-number estimate: **35%**.

That means: I’d put about a **35% probability that AGI is achieved by 2035 with LLM-descended systems as the central route**, by some lab or company.

A useful decomposition of my estimate would be roughly:

- **35%**: AGI by 2035 via LLMs / large multimodal foundation models / transformer-like successors with tool use, memory, RL, planning scaffolds, etc.
- **10%**: AGI by 2035 via a fundamentally different architecture.
- **55%**: no AGI by 2035.

So conditional on AGI happening by 2035, I’d guess there is around a **75–80% chance** it comes from the LLM/foundation-model lineage rather than a totally different paradigm.

Key reasons:

1. **LLMs are currently the dominant empirical path.**  
   Most frontier progress in general-purpose AI has come from scaling language/multimodal models, not from symbolic AI, neuroscience-inspired systems, robotics-first approaches, or other alternatives.

2. **“LLM” systems can absorb many missing pieces.**  
   Tool use, retrieval, long-term memory, planning modules, self-play, synthetic data, multimodal grounding, and RL can all be bolted onto or trained into LLM-style systems. So the route to AGI may not be “just next-token prediction,” but it could still be fundamentally LLM-derived.

3. **Economic and institutional momentum strongly favors this path.**  
   The biggest labs, compute clusters, talent pools, and commercial incentives are currently organized around scaling foundation models. That makes the LLM lineage more likely to be the first route if AGI arrives soon.

4. **But there are real bottlenecks.**  
   Current LLMs still have weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal reasoning, autonomous learning, grounding, and robust agency. If these require a deep architectural break rather than incremental extensions, the LLM route may fail.

5. **2035 is close but not absurdly close.**  
   A decade-ish is enough time for several generations of compute, data, algorithmic improvements, and agentic scaffolding. But it is also short enough that if current paradigms hit hard limits, a new architecture may not mature in time.

So I would not endorse “game over; AGI is not imminent” with high confidence. But I also would not say LLMs are clearly sufficient. My estimate is: **35% probability of LLM-route AGI by 2035**.

rows.answer:
**Best single-number estimate: 40%.**

By that I mean: **~40% chance that by the end of 2035, the first broadly recognized AGI is achieved through an LLM-centered/foundation-model lineage**—multimodal, tool-using, agentic, memory-augmented, RL/post-trained, maybe with verifiers or planners—rather than through a genuinely different paradigm.

My rough decomposition:

- **P(AGI by 2035): ~55%**
- **P(LLM-centered route | AGI by 2035): ~70–75%**
- Product: **~40%**

Key reasons **for** the LLM route:

1. **Empirical momentum:** The broadest general-purpose AI systems so far are LLM/foundation-model descendants.
2. **Incumbency:** The biggest labs, compute stacks, data pipelines, and deployment ecosystems are built around this paradigm.
3. **Multimodal + agentic extensions may be enough:** Many weaknesses—tool use, planning, memory, verification, long-context reasoning—may be solved by augmenting LLMs rather than replacing them.
4. **Alternatives are less visibly competitive:** Symbolic, neurosymbolic, RL-only, brain-inspired, and robotics-first approaches matter, but none currently shows the same generality-scaling profile.

Key reasons **against**:

1. **Reliability and long-horizon agency remain unsolved.**
2. **LLMs may lack robust world models, causal understanding, or continual learning in a way that scaling alone doesn’t fix.**
3. **Data/compute/economic/regulatory limits could slow the current path.**
4. **A new architecture could absorb LLM lessons but cease to be meaningfully “LLM-based.”**

If you mean **pure text next-token LLMs without major architectural scaffolding**, my estimate would be much lower—probably **<15%**. But for **LLM-descended foundation-model systems**, I’d put the by-2035 probability at about **40%**.

rows.answer:
**Best single-number estimate: 40%.**

I’m interpreting “via large language models” broadly: not a raw text-only chatbot, but an LLM/foundation-model-centered system augmented with multimodality, tools, memory, planning/search, RL, synthetic data, agents, and external verifiers. If you mean “plain next-token-prediction chatbot with no major scaffolding,” the probability would be much lower.

Why I put it around **40%**:

- **LLMs are currently the leading empirical path**: they already show broad competence across language, code, reasoning-like tasks, tool use, and multimodal understanding. If AGI arrives relatively soon, incumbents usually have the advantage.
- **Scaling plus system design may be enough**: many current weaknesses—hallucination, brittle reasoning, poor long-horizon planning, weak memory—may be mitigated by retrieval, self-checking, tool use, search, RL, environment interaction, and larger/more specialized models.
- **Investment and talent are overwhelmingly concentrated there**: most frontier labs are pushing LLM-family systems, so the probability mass is pulled toward that route.
- **But AGI by 2035 is far from guaranteed**: robust autonomous learning, causal/world modeling, long-term agency, reliability, and real-world adaptability remain open problems.
- **A different architecture could still win**: neurosymbolic systems, world-model/RL agents, brain-inspired architectures, or something not yet mainstream could prove necessary if LLMs hit a wall.
- **The boundary is blurry**: many future “non-LLM” systems may still use LLMs as a central reasoning/language/interface component, making the category distinction difficult.

So my view is: Gary Marcus may be right that vanilla LLMs alone are not the “royal road,” but I would not say “game over.” The most likely near-to-midterm AGI path, if AGI happens by 2035, still looks like an LLM-descended hybrid system. My absolute estimate for **AGI via LLM-centered systems by 2035** is **40%**.

rows.answer:
**My best single-number estimate: 40%** that AGI is achieved by the end of 2035 via an **LLM-family approach** by some lab/company.

By “LLM-family,” I’d include systems where the core intelligence is a large pretrained language/multimodal foundation model, even if heavily augmented with tools, memory, retrieval, RL, planning/search, code execution, agents, synthetic data, etc. I would *not* count a system whose central mechanism is a fundamentally different paradigm where language models are only a peripheral interface.

My rough decomposition:

| Outcome by end-2035 | Probability |
|---|---:|
| AGI via LLM/foundation-model lineage | **40%** |
| AGI via fundamentally different architecture | 15% |
| No AGI by 2035 | 45% |

So if you instead mean **conditional on AGI being achieved by 2035**, I’d put the chance that it comes from the LLM lineage at about **70–75%**.

Why my estimate is not lower:

- LLMs have shown unexpectedly broad generality: language, code, math, tool use, multimodal reasoning, instruction following.
- If AGI arrives within ~10 years, path dependence favors the current paradigm: the capital, talent, infrastructure, datasets, and deployment loops are overwhelmingly centered on LLM-style foundation models.
- Many apparent LLM weaknesses may be addressed by system-level additions: long context, memory, retrieval, verifiers, test-time compute, agents, RL, planning modules, and tool use.
- “LLM” is becoming less like “chatbot that predicts text” and more like “general sequence/action model trained on language, code, images, video, tools, and feedback.”

Why my estimate is not higher:

- Current systems are still brittle on long-horizon autonomy, factual reliability, causal reasoning, continual learning, and robust real-world planning.
- Scaling may hit harder limits from data quality, compute, energy, economics, or diminishing returns.
- AGI may require architectures with stronger built-in world models, active learning, persistent memory, embodiment, symbolic reasoning, or model-based RL.
- If LLMs plateau, it may take longer than 2035 for an alternative paradigm to mature.

I would not treat Gary Marcus-style “game over” claims as decisive. The real evidence supports uncertainty, not certainty. But I’d also avoid saying LLMs are obviously sufficient. My view is: **LLM-descended systems are currently the leading path to AGI by 2035, but the unconditional chance that they actually get there by then is around 40%.**

rows.answer:
**Best single-number estimate: 40%.**

By that I mean: roughly a **40% chance that AGI is achieved by 2035 and that the decisive system is recognizably on the LLM / foundation-model lineage**, rather than coming from a fundamentally different architecture.

My implicit decomposition is approximately:

- **~55%** chance of AGI by 2035 under a broad “can do most economically valuable cognitive work at or above human level” definition.
- **~70%** chance that, if AGI does arrive by 2035, it is built around LLM-like foundation models: large pretrained multimodal sequence models with tool use, memory, RL, search/planning scaffolds, synthetic data, etc.

So: **0.55 × 0.70 ≈ 40%**.

I would count as “via LLMs” a system where the core cognitive engine is a scaled language/multimodal foundation model, even if it uses retrieval, long-term memory, code execution, agents, verifiers, planners, robotics interfaces, or RL fine-tuning. I would *not* count a system where an LLM is merely a chat interface on top of a fundamentally separate symbolic, neuromorphic, model-based RL, or brain-emulation architecture.

Why my number is not lower:

- LLMs have already shown unusually broad generality across language, coding, reasoning, science, math, tool use, and multimodal tasks.
- The major labs, compute stacks, data pipelines, and investment are overwhelmingly aimed at extending this paradigm.
- Many apparent LLM weaknesses—memory, planning, reliability, tool use, factuality—may be addressable by scaffolding and training improvements without abandoning the paradigm.
- If AI systems start materially accelerating AI research, the current foundation-model path likely benefits first.

Why my number is not higher:

- Current LLMs remain brittle, hallucination-prone, weak at long-horizon autonomy, and not clearly grounded in causal world models.
- Data, compute, energy, and economics may slow scaling.
- AGI may require qualitatively different mechanisms: persistent world models, active learning, embodiment, symbolic abstraction, or model-based RL.
- By 2035, the winning system may be so hybridized that calling it “an LLM” becomes misleading.

So I disagree with the strong “game over” framing. But I also would not say LLMs are overwhelmingly certain to be the route. My best estimate: **40% unconditional probability by 2035; about 70% conditional on AGI arriving by then.**

rows.answer:
My best single-number estimate: **40%**.

That is an **unconditional** estimate: roughly a 40% chance that by the end of 2035 some lab/company achieves something reasonably called AGI and that the central route is **LLM/foundation-model-based**, not a fundamentally different architecture.

My rough decomposition:

| Outcome by 2035 | Probability |
|---|---:|
| AGI via LLM/foundation-model lineage | **40%** |
| AGI via fundamentally different architecture | 15% |
| No broadly accepted AGI by 2035 | 45% |

So if you meant **conditional on AGI being achieved by 2035**, my estimate would be about **70%** that it comes from the LLM/foundation-model lineage.

By “LLM route,” I would include systems that are not merely today’s chatbots: multimodal transformer/MoE models, tool-using agents, retrieval, long-term memory, RL/post-training, verifiers, search/planning modules, synthetic data, and agent scaffolding—so long as a large language/foundation model remains the central cognitive engine. I would not count a system where an LLM is just a front-end to a genuinely different cognitive architecture.

Why I put the number fairly high:

- **Empirical momentum:** LLM-like systems are currently the most general AI systems we have, and they keep absorbing adjacent capabilities: code, math, vision, tool use, robotics interfaces, and planning scaffolds.
- **Path dependence:** The talent, infrastructure, datasets, chips, benchmarks, and commercial incentives are overwhelmingly aimed at scaling and extending foundation models.
- **Hybridization is powerful:** Many apparent LLM weaknesses—memory, grounding, deliberation, verification, tool use—can plausibly be addressed by additions around an LLM core rather than by abandoning the paradigm.
- **2035 is close enough that incumbents matter:** In a 10-year-ish window, the leading paradigm has a big advantage over clean-sheet alternatives.

Why not higher:

- **Current LLMs are not AGI:** They remain brittle on long-horizon autonomy, causal reasoning, reliability, self-correction, continual learning, and grounded world modeling.
- **Scaling may hit walls:** Data quality, compute cost, energy, latency, and diminishing returns could slow progress.
- **AGI may need different learning loops:** Persistent world models, active exploration, embodiment, symbolic reasoning, or new RL/cognitive architectures may turn out to be essential.
- **Definitions matter:** If “AGI” means an autonomous system that can robustly replace top human researchers/engineers across months-long projects with little scaffolding, the probability is lower.

Bottom line: I think Gary Marcus is plausibly right that **today’s LLMs alone** are not the royal road to AGI. But I would not say “game over.” My estimate is that an **LLM-descended, heavily augmented foundation-model architecture** is still the most likely route if AGI arrives by 2035.

rows.answer:
My best single-number estimate: **40%**.

I’d interpret that as: **~40% probability that the first broadly recognized AGI by 2035 is built mainly on an LLM/foundation-model lineage**, rather than on a clearly different paradigm.

My rough decomposition:

- **P(AGI by 2035 at all): ~55%**
- **P(LLM-derived route wins, conditional on AGI by 2035): ~70–75%**
- Combined: **~0.55 × 0.72 ≈ 40%**

By “LLM-derived,” I’m including systems whose core is still a large transformer-like language/multimodal model, possibly with RL, tool use, memory, retrieval, planning modules, verifiers, synthetic data, agents, or code execution. I’m **not** counting a totally different route such as a brain-like architecture, a symbolic cognitive architecture, or a non-language-centered embodied RL system.

Why not lower?

- LLMs are currently the most general AI systems we have.
- They already handle language, code, reasoning-ish behavior, multimodal inputs, tool use, and agent scaffolding.
- The world’s top labs are heavily invested in scaling and extending this paradigm.
- If AGI arrives soon, path dependence makes it likely to come from the current dominant approach.
- Many alleged LLM weaknesses — memory, planning, factuality, tool use, long-horizon agency — may be addressable by adding systems around the model rather than replacing the paradigm.

Why not higher?

- “AGI by 2035” itself is far from guaranteed.
- Current LLMs still have serious weaknesses in robustness, causal reasoning, autonomy, continual learning, and long-horizon planning.
- Data, compute, energy, and economics may impose harder limits than optimists expect.
- A future breakthrough may involve architectures that look only loosely related to today’s LLMs.
- If “LLM” means “just scale next-token-prediction chatbots,” then the probability is much lower — maybe **10–15%**.

So my headline view is:

> **AGI via LLM-descended systems by 2035: ~40%.**

I’d say Gary Marcus-style skepticism is directionally useful as a warning against overclaiming, but “game over” seems much too strong. The LLM route is not guaranteed, but it remains the leading candidate for near-to-medium-term AGI.

rows.answer:
My best single-number estimate: **40%**.

That is my estimate for the unconditional probability that **AGI is achieved by 2035 via an LLM/foundation-model-centered route**, by any lab or company.

I’m interpreting “via LLMs” broadly: not a plain text-only chatbot, but a system whose core intelligence comes from scaled language/multimodal foundation models, plus tools, memory, RL, search, agents, synthetic data, etc. If you mean “a vanilla next-token text LLM with no major scaffolding,” my estimate would be much lower, probably **<10%**.

A rough decomposition:

| Outcome by 2035 | My probability |
|---|---:|
| AGI achieved, LLM/foundation-model-centered | **40%** |
| AGI achieved, fundamentally different architecture | **15%** |
| No AGI by 2035 | **45%** |

So conditional on AGI happening by 2035, I’d put the chance that it comes through the LLM/foundation-model lineage at about **70%**.

Why not lower?

- LLMs already show broad transfer across language, code, math, planning, tool use, vision-language, and scientific tasks.
- The paradigm is highly extensible: retrieval, memory, tool use, self-play, inference-time search, multimodality, robotics interfaces, and agent scaffolding can be layered onto the core model.
- Scaling laws and inference-time compute still appear useful, even if raw pretraining scaling slows.
- Most frontier-lab talent, infrastructure, data pipelines, and investment are currently organized around this lineage.
- If AGI happens soon, path dependence matters: the winning system is more likely to evolve from the dominant paradigm than from a clean-sheet alternative.

Why not higher?

- Current LLMs still have serious weaknesses in reliability, causal understanding, long-horizon autonomy, continual learning, grounding, and robust self-correction.
- It is plausible that true AGI needs qualitatively different mechanisms: world models, embodied learning, model-based RL, neuro-symbolic reasoning, active scientific experimentation, or architectures with persistent agency and memory baked in.
- Data, compute, energy, regulation, and safety constraints could slow the LLM trajectory.
- “LLM-based AGI” may become a fuzzy category if future systems are hybrids; at some point, the LLM may be only one component rather than the central engine.

Bottom line: I would not say “game over.” The Marcus-style critique identifies real limitations, but I don’t think it establishes that LLMs are a dead end. My best guess is that **LLM-descended systems are currently the leading route to AGI by 2035**, but the total probability is still well below certainty.

Why not higher:

rows.answer:
**My best single-number estimate: 40%.**

That is, I’d put about a **40% probability** on **AGI being achieved by 2035 via the LLM / large multimodal foundation-model lineage**, by any lab or company.

My interpretation:

- **“AGI”** = a system that can autonomously perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly human-expert level or better, not necessarily conscious or embodied.
- **“Via LLMs”** = not just a frozen chatbot doing next-token prediction, but a descendant of today’s LLM stack: large-scale self-supervised language/multimodal models, likely with tools, memory, RL, synthetic data, verifiers, planning loops, agents, etc.
- If you mean **“pure text-only next-token transformer becomes AGI with no major additions,”** I’d put that much lower, maybe **10–15%**.

A rough decomposition:

- Probability of AGI by 2035 at all: **~55%**
- Conditional probability that, if AGI arrives by 2035, it comes mainly from the LLM/foundation-model lineage rather than a fundamentally different architecture: **~70%**
- Joint probability: **0.55 × 0.70 ≈ 40%**

Why not lower:

- LLMs have already shown surprisingly broad generalization across language, code, math, tool use, multimodal tasks, and instruction following.
- The current frontier-AI ecosystem is overwhelmingly built around scaling and extending foundation models.
- “LLM” systems are rapidly becoming compound systems: model + tools + memory + retrieval + agents + verifiers + RL. That may be enough to overcome many present weaknesses.
- The bitter-lesson argument still has force: general methods plus scale have repeatedly beaten hand-designed alternatives.

Why not higher:

- Current LLMs still struggle with reliability, causal reasoning, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and robust autonomy.
- Data, compute, energy, and economics may become serious bottlenecks.
- A future successful system may require architectures closer to model-based RL, active world-model learning, neurosymbolic reasoning, or something not recognizable as an LLM.
- “AGI” may require a level of autonomous adaptation and error correction that current LLM-style training does not naturally provide.

So my bottom line: **Gary Marcus may be right that today’s plain LLMs are not a straight royal road to AGI. But I would not call it game over. The LLM/foundation-model lineage is still, in my view, the leading candidate path.**

**Final estimate: 40%.**

rows.answer:
**Best single-number estimate: 40%.**

I’m interpreting the question as: **“By the end of 2035, what is the probability that AGI is first achieved by an LLM/foundation-model-centered approach, as opposed to a fundamentally different paradigm?”** Under that interpretation, my estimate is **~40%**.

If you instead mean **conditional on AGI being achieved by 2035**, I’d put the probability that it comes from the LLM/foundation-model lineage higher, around **65–75%**. The difference is that my 40% includes the chance that no one achieves AGI by 2035 at all.

My assumptions:

- **“Via LLMs”** does *not* mean a vanilla next-token-predicting chatbot. It includes large transformer/foundation-model systems with multimodality, tool use, long-term memory, search, code execution, reinforcement learning, agent scaffolding, synthetic data, and verifier/reasoner modules.
- **“Fundamentally different architectures”** means something not primarily descended from today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm: e.g. a new neurosymbolic, brain-inspired, model-based RL, or other architecture where LLMs are only peripheral.

Why I’m not very low:

1. **Current LLMs already show broad generality.** They are flawed, but the range of cognitive tasks they can perform is unusually wide compared with prior AI paradigms.
2. **The frontier labs are heavily invested in this path.** OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta, xAI and others are mostly extending the foundation-model approach, not abandoning it.
3. **“LLM” systems are becoming more than text models.** Tool use, multimodal perception, memory, planning loops, code execution, self-play/synthetic data and RL could patch many current weaknesses without requiring a totally new paradigm.
4. **The first AGI, if near-term, is likely to be messy and hybrid.** It may look like “LLM + agents + tools + learned world models + verifiers,” but the core intelligence engine may still be recognizably LLM/foundation-model-derived.

Why I’m not very high:

1. **Reliability remains a major obstacle.** Hallucination, brittleness, weak long-horizon planning, inconsistent reasoning, and poor self-correction are not trivial defects.
2. **Scaling may hit diminishing returns.** More compute and data may continue to help, but it is not guaranteed that scaling current methods yields robust autonomous general intelligence.
3. **AGI may require better continual learning, grounding, agency, causal modeling or world modeling** than current LLM-centered systems naturally provide.
4. **A successor architecture may absorb LLM lessons but no longer be “an LLM.”** For example, a system based on learned simulators, active inference, model-based RL, or neuro-symbolic reasoning could become dominant before 2035.

So my bottom line is:

**Probability AGI is achieved by 2035 via an LLM/foundation-model-centered path: ~40%.**

But **conditional on AGI happening by 2035**, I think the odds favor the LLM lineage over a cleanly different paradigm.

**Best single-number estimate: ~45%.**

That is my **unconditional** estimate that by the end of 2035, at least one lab/company achieves a broadly recognized AGI whose core cognitive engine is **LLM/large-multimodal-model–descended**, rather than a fundamentally different architecture.

Rough decomposition:

- **P(AGI by 2035): ~60%**
- **P(LLM-descended route | AGI by 2035): ~75%**
- Combined: **0.60 × 0.75 ≈ 45%**

By “LLM-descended,” I do **not** mean a plain 2024-style chatbot with prompt engineering. I mean a system whose central model is a Transformer/foundation-model successor trained on language/code/multimodal data, likely augmented with RL, tool use, retrieval, memory, planning/search, agents, verifiers, simulators, and synthetic data. If you mean “pure text-only next-token LLM with no major architectural/scaffolding additions,” my estimate would be much lower, maybe **10–15%**.

Why 45%, not near zero:

- LLMs already show unusually broad transfer across language, code, reasoning, multimodal tasks, and tool use.
- The world’s strongest AI labs are investing heavily in this paradigm, so the search process is biased toward LLM-like successors.
- Many apparent weaknesses—memory, planning, factuality, tool use, long-horizon agency—may be solvable by hybrid systems around foundation models rather than by replacing them.
- Language/code may be a very efficient interface for general intelligence because much human knowledge and reasoning is compressed into it.
- If AGI arrives soon, evolutionary improvement of the current dominant paradigm seems more likely than a clean-sheet paradigm shift.

Why not 80–90%:

- Current LLMs still have serious issues with reliability, causal understanding, persistent goals, autonomous learning, and long-horizon execution.
- Scaling may hit harder data, compute, or economics limits than optimists expect.
- AGI may require stronger world models, active learning, embodiment, neurosymbolic reasoning, or model-based RL architectures that are not merely “LLMs plus tools.”
- The category boundary is fuzzy: a 2035 “LLM-based AGI” may look very different from today’s LLMs.

So my bottom line: **Gary Marcus may be right that today’s LLMs are not sufficient, but I think “game over” is much too strong. My best estimate is ~45% that AGI by 2035 comes through the LLM/foundation-model lineage.**
